cross_system.title: Cross-system signal: credit_card flow softening ahead of stock
cross_system.body: Credit card spending is slowing down, which usually happens before credit card loans start shrinking. When people spend less on their cards, they typically pay down their balances over the next few months, causing total credit card debt to drop. This gives banks a few months' head start to see what's coming.
cross_system.implication: Banks should prepare for lower credit card loan growth and adjust their lending plans before the slowdown hits their loan books.
chain: People are spending less money on their credit cards
Lower spending usually leads to people paying down their card balances over the next few months
Banks can spot this early and adjust their credit card lending before loan growth actually slows

sibc.title: Co-lending & warehouse-financing infrastructure
sibc.body: Small business credit is growing at 30.09% this year and now represents 23.12% of all business lending. The government's UDYAM registration and GST data are making tiny businesses easier to check, and growth in micro and small business lending has accelerated by 23.77 percentage points. Banks find it hard to reach thousands of small borrowers directly, so they need partners who can do the ground work.
sibc.implication: Banks should fund NBFCs and fintech lenders who specialize in small business loans, using co-lending agreements or warehouse credit lines, because these partners can reach and assess tiny borrowers that banks cannot serve efficiently on their own.
chain: UDYAM registration and GST data make small businesses formally visible and checkable for the first time
Small business lending is growing at 30.09% because lenders can now verify creditworthiness using this new data
Banks cannot handle thousands of tiny loans directly, so they should finance specialist lenders who do the on-ground work and use the new data to pick good borrowers
sibc._driver: MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data

sibc.title: Gold loan market entry / deepening for banks
sibc.body: Banks are growing gold loans by 121.21 percent compared to last year, and this growth has continued for 8.0 periods in a row. Gold prices jumped between 2023 and 2026, so people who own gold can now borrow much more money against it. Banks are winning customers from finance companies because banks have local branches where staff can check the gold is real and keep it safe, plus banks charge lower interest rates.
sibc.implication: A bank should open or expand gold loan counters at branches, train staff to test gold purity quickly, and advertise lower rates than finance companies to pull in these borrowers while gold stays expensive.
chain: Gold prices rose sharply from 2023 to 2026, making each piece of gold worth much more as collateral
Banks can lend bigger amounts against the same gold and do it more safely than finance companies because they have local branches to verify and store the gold
Borrowers switch to banks for better rates and convenience, driving the 121.21 percent yearly growth that has lasted 8.0 periods

sibc.title: MSME origination infrastructure investment window
sibc.body: Small business lending is growing at 30.09 pct per year, and these tiny businesses now make up 23.12 pct of all MSME lending. Their growth rate jumped by 23.77 pp recently, reaching 32.68 pct, while bigger MSME borrowers are growing 20.06 pp slower. Banks using government registration systems and GST records to assess these small businesses are capturing this boom.
sibc.implication: Build digital underwriting systems now that use UDYAM registration and GST data to assess small businesses, because you have roughly 2-3 years before this 30.09 pct growth opportunity becomes too competitive.
chain: Government formalization through UDYAM and GST is making tiny businesses visible and assessable for the first time
Banks with digital systems to read this government data can now lend to a huge new pool of 23.12 pct of the MSME market growing at 30.09 pct
Banks without these systems will miss this window as competitors lock in relationships with these fast-growing small borrowers
sibc._driver: MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data

sibc.title: PLI anchor-supplier chain finance
sibc.body: The government is paying big manufacturers to expand production under the PLI scheme, and those manufacturers need more suppliers. Lending to suppliers in basic metals grew 16.65 pct and 19.91 pct, chemicals grew 20.6 pct and 19.36 pct, and engineering grew 34.51 pct. These suppliers are safer to lend to because they have contracts with large established companies that guarantee payment.
sibc.implication: Banks should lend more to suppliers in these industries, especially engineering where growth hit 34.51 pct, because the contracts with big manufacturers reduce the risk of non-payment.
chain: Government pays big manufacturers to expand under PLI, so those manufacturers need more parts and materials from suppliers
Suppliers get contracts with large established companies, which makes them safer borrowers because payment is more certain
Banks can lend more confidently to these suppliers, especially in fast-growing areas like engineering

sibc.title: PSLC market depth from PSL reclassification surplus
sibc.body: Housing loans that count toward priority sector lending jumped 39.94% year-over-year after RBI raised the ceiling in October 2024. Banks did not lend more money; the rule change simply let them reclassify existing housing loans as priority sector loans. This created instant surplus PSL credits that banks can now sell to other banks who are short on their required priority sector targets.
sibc.implication: Buy PSL certificates from banks with reclassification surplus at a discount, because they got these credits for free and will sell cheap to monetize them before other banks flood the market with the same surplus.
chain: RBI raised the housing loan ceiling for priority sector lending in October 2024, so loans that did not count before suddenly counted
Banks with large housing portfolios instantly had extra PSL credits without making any new loans
Banks holding this free surplus will sell PSL certificates quickly and cheaply to turn the windfall into cash
sibc._driver: RBI PSL housing loan ceiling revision (Oct 2024)

sibc.title: Renewable energy project finance — next capex wave
sibc.body: Old infrastructure lending is slowing down — some sectors fell -26.03% and -44.06% this year, while new renewable energy sectors jumped 74.02% and 21.47%. Banks already have teams that know how to finance big, long-term projects like roads and power plants. Government renewable energy targets mean solar, wind, and green hydrogen projects now need the same kind of financing.
sibc.implication: Banks should move their project finance teams from old infrastructure to renewable energy projects, using the same skills but on new deals.
chain: Traditional infrastructure projects are finishing and paying back loans, so those sectors are shrinking
Government is pushing renewable energy targets, creating new solar, wind, and hydrogen projects that need big, long-term loans
Banks can use their existing project finance teams and skills to lend to these new renewable projects instead
sibc._via: Project lifecycle phase → new origination vs repayment

sibc.title: Services sector credit entry / scale-up
sibc.body: Services sector lending is growing at 18.58% per year, much faster than most other types of loans, and now represents 28.21% of all bank credit. Banks are increasingly lending to NBFCs that specialize in services loans, and this channel is growing at 16.68% to 57.1% after regulators eased some rules. Some specific services sub-sectors are growing extremely fast — one at 52.75%, another at 40.7%, and another at 27.73%.
sibc.implication: A bank should build or expand its services lending book, either by lending directly to services companies or by partnering with NBFCs that already know this sector well, because this is where credit demand is strongest right now.
chain: Regulators changed the rules on how banks can lend to NBFCs, making it easier and cheaper again
Banks responded by increasing loans to NBFCs that focus on services companies, and that flow grew 16.68% to 57.1%
Services credit overall is now growing at 18.58% and taking a bigger share of total lending at 28.21%, so banks that enter now can ride this wave
sibc._driver: NBFC credit channel recovery post risk-weight cycle

sibc.title: EV-specific vehicle credit products
sibc.body: RETIRED at first FOUNDATION (2026-06-10). No external source verified for EV share of vehicle loans or EV-specific credit risk differentiation. No L1 signal separating EV from ICE vehicle loans. Returns only when EV share of vehicle loans is measurable in L1 (e.g., VAHAN registration data ingestion).
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.

sibc.title: Infrastructure repayment wave with insufficient new origination pipeline
sibc.body: Banks over-indexed to traditional infrastructure credit face a structural squeeze: the FY22-24 project book moves to repayment (compressing balances) while the next-cycle pipeline (renewables, data centres) is slower to ramp. Banks without next-cycle project finance capability face credit portfolio contraction.
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: As a project cohort moves from construction (drawdown) to operations (repayment), net credit balance declines unless new origination offsets it — suppressing growth even while existing books remain large.
This is being driven by Infrastructure supercycle completing (FY22–24).
sibc._via: Project lifecycle phase → new origination vs repayment

sibc.title: CIC reporting compliance gaps creating off-balance-sheet credit risk
sibc.body: RBI Digital Lending Guidelines (2022/2025) require BNPL and digital credit to report to Credit Information Companies. However, compliance may be incomplete among newer digital lenders operating under first-loss guarantee structures or co-lending arrangements where the regulated entity's on-book exposure is partially shielded. If reporting gaps exist, the true unsecured credit stress is understated in bank balance sheets and in SIBC data.
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Higher risk weights raise the regulatory capital cost per unit of exposure, compressing origination economics for unsecured retail products until reversed.
This is being driven by RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023).
sibc._driver: RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023)
sibc._via: Regulatory capital weight → unsecured origination economics

atm_pos.title: Credit card penetration deepening — PSB share gap is the opening
atm_pos.body: Credit cards are growing at 8.15 percent per year, with 119436129.0 total cards in the market. Public sector banks hold only 23.8 percent of all credit cards, but they control a much larger share of debit cards, meaning they already have millions of customers who trust them for banking but haven't been offered credit cards. Private banks have 71.01 percent of the credit card market, showing public sector banks are missing a huge opportunity with their own customer base.
atm_pos.implication: Public sector banks should launch simple credit card programs targeting their existing debit card customers, because selling to people who already bank with you costs far less than finding completely new customers.
chain: Public sector banks have many debit card customers but very few credit card customers compared to private banks
These existing customers already trust the bank and have accounts, so offering them credit cards is much cheaper than finding strangers
By converting even a small portion of their own customers, public sector banks can grow their credit card business quickly without heavy marketing costs

atm_pos.title: Rural cash distribution via Micro ATM / Business Correspondent networks
atm_pos.body: Micro ATMs fell by -7.17 percent, but there are still 1367989.0 of them across rural India. Rural customers need cash, yet full ATMs and branches cost too much to run in villages. That leaves over a million micro ATMs as the only practical way to deliver cash in these areas.
atm_pos.implication: Banks should partner with business correspondents who run these 1367989.0 micro ATMs, offering cash-management loans and working-capital credit so the network stays alive and reaches customers no branch can serve.
chain: Micro ATMs dropped -7.17 percent but 1367989.0 units still operate in villages where full branches are too expensive
Rural customers have no other way to withdraw cash, so these devices remain the only link to banking
Banks can lend to the agents running these machines, keeping the network funded and expanding rural deposit and loan reach

atm_pos.title: Embedded transit and tap-to-pay payment products
atm_pos.body: Credit card spending in the 'other' category jumped 492.78% in value and 299.83% in volume this year. This explosion comes from people now using credit cards to pay for metro rides, bus tickets, and quick tap purchases at stores—things they used to pay for with cash or debit cards. New payment systems that let you tap your credit card on a metro gate or at a shop counter are making this possible.
atm_pos.implication: Banks should push credit cards hard into transit systems and small everyday merchants, because people are ready to switch from cash to cards for these routine purchases.
chain: New tap-to-pay systems are installed in metros, buses, and small shops
People find it easier to tap their credit card than fumble with cash or debit cards
Everyday spending that was never on credit cards before now flows through them, creating huge growth in a new category
atm_pos._driver: Embedded card payment infrastructure rollout (transit NFC or RuPay-credit-on-UPI)
atm_pos._via: Embedded payment rails → new card-spend surface

atm_pos.title: Credit card market concentrated in top-5 private banks
atm_pos.body: The top-5 private banks control approximately 71% of credit card outstanding. The largest single issuer holds ~20% of the market. This concentration creates systemic fragility — as demonstrated when a single payment bank's exit caused a market-wide debit card step-down. Similar disruption from a major credit card issuer would have proportionally larger effects.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.

atm_pos.title: Debit card base structural decline reducing cash access for underbanked
atm_pos.body: The debit card base is contracting through payment bank exits and lifecycle purges. This reduces the potential transaction base for populations that depend on debit cards for cash access and digital payments — particularly lower-income and rural segments without credit cards or reliable UPI-linked accounts.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Regulatory wind-down of a major issuer forces cancellation of its entire card portfolio, producing a single-period step-down in the stock concentrated in that issuer's lender category.
This is being driven by RBI enforcement action against Paytm Payments Bank (Jan–Apr 2026).
atm_pos._driver: RBI enforcement action against Paytm Payments Bank (Jan–Apr 2026)

atm_pos.title: Payment acceptance bifurcation: POS contracting while UPI grows
atm_pos.body: POS terminal network contraction while UPI QR expands creates an acceptance gap: merchants who drop POS terminals cannot accept international cards, EMI transactions, premium credit card features, or POS-authenticated payments. Customers without UPI-linked accounts face reduced options at merchants who have switched fully to UPI.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Zero MDR on UPI makes QR acceptance structurally cheaper than card POS (no hardware, no per-txn fee), driving QR deployment while suppressing POS-terminal investment. Polarity is per-target: + for QR, − for POS.
This is being driven by Zero MDR policy on UPI transactions (NPCI/GoI).
atm_pos._driver: Zero MDR policy on UPI transactions (NPCI/GoI)
